mappings.rationaleAI: Constantine's legalization of Christianity, founding of Constantinople, and eastward decentralization of the Empire directly address C.1 (origins of Byzantium in the late Roman Empire: the reforms of Diocletian and Constantine).

Julian's attempt to reverse Christianization fifty years after Constantine illuminates the religious and political tensions of the late Roman Empire that shaped Byzantium's formation, relevant to C.1 (origins of Byzantium in the late Roman Empire).

Arianism's adoption by Ostrogoths, Vandals, and Visigoths and its conflict with Catholic Christianity across late antique and early medieval Europe directly addresses B.1.b (the issue of Arianism versus Catholic Christianity among the Germanic successor states).

Hilda's abbacy at Whitby, the Synod of Whitby deciding Roman versus Celtic Easter dating, and production of five bishops map to B.3.a (conversion to Catholic Christianity and religious/cultural functions of monasticism) and B.2.b.ii (Anglo-Saxon kingdoms in England and Celtic kingdoms).

Cuthbert's role as monk, bishop of Lindisfarne, and advisor to Northumbrian kings embodies B.3.a (conversion and religious/cultural functions of monasticism in early medieval England) and B.2.b.ii (Anglo-Saxon kingdoms), representing the intersection of Celtic and Roman Christian traditions.

Produced c.700 on Holy Island following the Gregorian mission to convert Anglo-Saxon Britain, the Lindisfarne Gospels exemplify B.3.a (conversion to Catholic Christianity and monastic cultural functions) and B.3.b (arts, intellectual life, and education in the early Middle Ages), blending Celtic, Anglo-Saxon, and Mediterranean artistic traditions.

Charles Martel's 732 defeat of an Arab-Berber army at Tours addresses B.2.b.iii (rise of the Carolingians under Charles Martel) and B.2.c (effects of the rise of Islam on western Europe), marking both Carolingian consolidation of Frankish power and the halting of Muslim military expansion into northern Europe.

Charlemagne's cultural and literary revival, monastic scriptoria preserving classical texts, standardized Latin script, and cathedral schools map to D.1.a (patronage of arts and learning under Charlemagne) and D.1.b.ii (the Carolingian renaissance in literature and the arts).

Justinian I's sixth-century Corpus Juris Civilis, compiling centuries of Roman legal precedent into a systematic codification, maps precisely to C.2.d (the reign of Justinian I and the Code of Justinian).

The Sassanid Empire as Byzantium's principal rival provides essential context for C.2.f (relations with the Persians under Justinian's successors) and C.3.a (Heraclius's wars with Persia), with the Sassanid fall to the Islamic conquests directly precipitating the Arab threat to Byzantium.

The seventh-century Arab conquests of the Byzantine Levant and North Africa directly address C.3.a (loss of Syria, Palestine, Armenia, and Egypt to the Arabs under Heraclius) and B.2.c (effects of the rise of Islam on western Europe).

A broad examination of Byzantine culture and history from the 395 division through its thousand-year survival covers sections C (early Byzantine Empire) and F (Byzantine zenith and decline), while the historiographic discussion of why the East survived when the West fell addresses A (historical sources and historiographic problems).

mappings.episodeTitle: Alfred and the Battle of Edington
mappings.rationaleAI: Alfred's 878 victory over the Vikings at Edington and his subsequent project to fashion English identity directly address D.2.a (the 9th-century Danish invasions and King Alfred's legal, administrative, and ecclesiastical policies).

The partition of ninth-century England between Anglo-Saxon and Viking territories, the Great Heathen Army's conquests, and Alfred's negotiated settlement with Guthrum directly address D.2.a (the 9th-century Danish invasions and King Alfred's policies).
Athelstan's unification of England, legal reforms, and foreign diplomacy as grandson of Alfred the Great directly address D.2.a (Anglo-Saxon political unification in the 10th century).
Cnut's consolidation of a North Sea empire spanning England, Denmark, and Norway as Danish king of England addresses D.2.a (the conquest of the Danes and their rule over the Anglo-Danish state).

Harold Godwinson's 1066 defeat of Harald Hardrada's Norwegian invasion, ending the Viking military threat to England just before the Norman Conquest, addresses D.2.a (the Norman Conquest) and D.2.c (the Viking Age's widespread raids and conquests).

William the Conqueror's 1086 survey of English landholding documents the transformation of Anglo-Saxon England following the Norman Conquest, the culminating event of D.2.a, providing primary evidence for feudal social structure and Norman governance.

The Picts' inhabitation of Scotland from 300-900 AD, their resistance to Roman expansion, carved symbol stones, and eventual merger with Gaelic-speaking Scots under Kenneth MacAlpin address D.2.b.i (Roman penetration in Scotland, Christianity, and Norse influence).

The sagas depict Norse settlement of Iceland from the late ninth century, covering social structures, law, blood feuds, and settlement patterns that illuminate D.2.c (the Viking Age in Scandinavia: social and political organization, arts, paganism and conversion to Christianity).

Norse eastward expansion along Russian river systems, trade with Constantinople and Baghdad, and the founding of settlements that became Kievan Rus map to E.2.c (exploration and rise of the Rus, trade routes), E.2.e (Slavic-Varangian origins of Kievan Rus), and D.2.c (Viking Age raids and conquests).

The Almoravids' crossing into Iberia to halt the Christian Reconquista, their defeat of El Cid, and temporary unification of Islamic North Africa and southern Spain directly address D.5 (growth of Christian states in northern Spain, their relations with Muslims, and the first phase of the Reconquista).

Venice's founding by refugees fleeing barbarian invasions and its rise as a maritime power controlling eastern Mediterranean trade routes is directly addressed in the section F heading (the growth of Venice alongside Byzantine zenith and decline).

The 1054 Great Schism dividing Christianity into Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox communions over papal authority and the filioque clause maps directly to F.3 (Byzantine decline, the schism with Rome in 1054).

Alcuin served at Charlemagne's court in Aachen, playing a central role in the Carolingian Renaissance (A.3.a the Carolingian dynasty and the empire of Charlemagne). The development of Carolingian script at Tours connects to Carolingian cultural achievements (A.3.b).

The poem was composed in Anglo-Saxon England during the early Middle Ages, connecting to the Germanic kingdoms in Britain (A.2.b the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms) and their cultural and literary achievements (A.2.c).

The saga covers the Viking Age in Scandinavia (D.2.c) with widespread Norse raids, conquests, and settlement patterns, and also relates to the development of Scotland (D.2.b) given Orkney's position between Norwegian and Scottish crowns.

Merlin's origins in Celtic bardic tradition and the early post-Roman British context connect to religion, arts, and society in the early Middle Ages (B.3.a) and the amalgamation of late Classical and Germanic cultures (B.3.b).

mappings.episodeTitle: The Roman Empire's Collapse in the 5th century
mappings.rationaleAI: The end of the Western Roman Empire and the Germanic Volkerwanderung (B.1.a), including the invasions of Ostrogoths, Visigoths, and Vandals, and the development of Germanic successor states (B.1.b) are directly covered.
The 541 AD plague struck during Justinian I's reign (C.2.d covers Justinian's era), devastating Constantinople and the broader Byzantine Empire.

The Battle of Clontarf (1014), Brian Boru's defeat of Hiberno-Norse forces, and the Norse kingdoms of Dublin relate to Ireland during the Norse invasions (D.2.b.iii) and the Viking Age in Scandinavia with its widespread raids and conquests (D.2.c).

The poem is set during Charlemagne's Iberian campaigns in the late eighth century; the Carolingian empire and Charlemagne's military expeditions (B.2.a); the cultural achievements of the Carolingian period inform the poem's historical backdrop (B.3)
The tale derives from Celtic mythology reaching written form in post-Norman Conquest Britain; its literary elaboration occurred during the early medieval cultural developments in Western Europe (B.3)
